title: Not So Brilliant
subtitle: Why 100% Attendance isn't a helpful goal
paragraphs: On a toilet door in my local town, I’m confronted by a sticker.
Brilliant! it tells me. 100% Attendance This Week.
And I’m reminded of the constant mantra families hear around them.

I’m reminded of the ‘Meet the Tutor’ sessions where I’ve been told that every moment of class time matters, and my son’s future will be blighted (and his exam results affected) by every minute he misses. I’m reminded of the ‘Improved Attenders’ party at the local primary school, where those who have the luck not to get sick are celebrated with balloons and supermarket cake. I’m reminded of the assemblies where those with 100% attendance are rewarded with book tokens and their photographs posted on the school noticeboard.

And I’m reminded of the teenagers I meet who have been excluded from their end-of-school prom, for poor attendance. Those left behind when everyone else heads off to the theme park for their end of year ‘Reward Trip’, only available for those with 95% attendance or above. The children who become distraught at the news that they have covid and who beg to go to school anyway, because that’s their 100% attendance record blown for the entire year.

It’s all based on a lie. Attendance is not enough and it’s no guarantee of success. It’s possible to attend school every day and learn nothing, and it’s possible to not go to school at all and learn loads. More school doesn’t always equal more learning, but we’re telling children that it does.
This leads to hopelessness in those who struggle to attend. It leads to exclusion of those who most need to be included. It makes it harder for those who have been absent to return.

Our children need a different message. They need to hear that school is one way to learn, but there are others. They need to hear that turning up is never enough, and its what you do next that matters. They need to hear that they will have a future and that they belong, even if they can’t get to school every day.

Widespread attendance problems tell us that something isn’t right – but we’re looking in the wrong place. The problem isn’t children and families. Our schools aren’t working for many children and compelling them to attend won’t change that.
We have to look to the system first, and ask ourselves whether our schools are places that children want to be. If not, let’s start there.
